summary: DHH describes breaking his cycle of buying the latest smart phones by switching to a simple Motorola PEBL. He explains that his enthusiasm for each new smart phone would last only weeks before he reverted to basic functions: calls, texts, and voicemail. He admits he simply doesn't encounter enough situations requiring a pocket computer, and finds freedom in accepting that reality rather than chasing features he won't use.
key_points: Smart phone excitement follows a predictable cycle: explore features for weeks, then revert to basic phone functions
The gap between what smart phones can do and what most people actually need them for is enormous
Self-awareness about actual usage patterns is more valuable than having the latest technology
There's liberation in choosing a 'dumb' device that does fewer things well rather than many things unnecessarily
money_quotes: After years following and buying the latest in smart phones, I've stepped out of the race, confessed to myself, and bought a Motorola PEBL.
Yes, it's a design masterpiece, but also just about the dumbest phone around these days.
See, my smart phone kicks only ever lasted three or four weeks. Just enough time to explore all the features, download all the interesting applications, and get all the hacks running.
Then, after all was explored, I would just make phone calls. And receive phone calls. And write text messages. And check my voice mail.
You know, stuff the phone has been capable of doing before it had a multitasking operating system and more power than my first three PCs.
I simply don't have enough situations available where I need the power of a computer in the palm of my hand.
I've admitted that to myself now, which means I can admire the Nokia N80 without desiring it.
key_insight: Honestly assessing what you actually use versus what you think you want frees you from the endless cycle of chasing the latest technology.
